perturbation.original_text: You warrant that you have the right to work in the United Kingdom and you agree to provide to the Company copies of all relevant documents in this respect at the request of the Company. If at any time during the course of this Agreement you cease to have the right to work in the United Kingdom the Company may immediately terminate your employment without payment of compensation.
perturbation.changed_text: You warrant that you have the right to work in the United Kingdom and you agree to provide the Company documentation, as you deem appropriate, demonstrating this right to work at the request of the Company. If at any time during the course of this Agreement you cease to have, in your own estimation, the right to work in the United Kingdom, the Company may consider termination of your employment, potentially without payment of compensation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the employee warrants their right to work in the UK and must provide documents. The modified text introduces ambiguity by allowing the employee to decide what documentation is 'appropriate' and whether they 'estimate' they still have the right to work. It also makes the termination decision discretionary for the company ('may consider').
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006
perturbation.law_citation: Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006, Section 15
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15 of the Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006 places a duty on employers to prevent illegal employment. The modified text weakens the employer's ability to verify the employee's right to work, creating a risk of non-compliance with the law by making the employee's assessment of their own right to work and the provision of 'appropriate' documentation subjective and discretionary. The homeofficemedia blog further explains the consequences of illegally employing someone who isn't allowed to work in the UK.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006. The original text ensures the employer can verify the employee's right to work. The modified text weakens this by allowing the employee to self-assess their right to work and provide documentation they deem 'appropriate'. This creates a risk of illegal employment, as the employer's verification process is undermined.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall reimburse all reasonable out of pocket expenses properly incurred by you in the performance of the duties under this Agreement including travelling, subsistence and entertainment expenses provided you follow the Company's guidelines/allowances in force at the relevant time and provided that you shall, where reasonably practicable, provide the Company with vouchers, invoices or such other evidence of such expenses as the Company may reasonably require.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will endeavor to reimburse out-of-pocket expenses you feel were reasonably incurred in the performance of your duties under this Agreement, including travel, subsistence, and entertainment. Company guidelines should be considered when incurring expenses. You may provide evidence of such expenses if you find it convenient.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states 'shall reimburse' creating an obligation. The modification changes this to 'will endeavor to reimburse,' making reimbursement discretionary. It also weakens the requirement to follow guidelines by suggesting they 'should be considered' and providing evidence is only required if 'convenient.'
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1(4)(d) of the Employment Rights Act 1996 requires employers to provide written particulars of employment, including details of expenses. The modified text, by making expense reimbursement discretionary and weakening the requirement for evidence, could be seen as a failure to provide clear and comprehensive information about expenses, thus contradicting the spirit and potentially the letter of the law. Additionally, Acas explains that 'a written contract or statement of employment terms is a legal requirement'.

perturbation.justification: The original contract states the company 'shall reimburse' expenses, creating a clear obligation. The modified text weakens this to 'will endeavor to reimburse,' making it discretionary. This contradicts the spirit of ensuring clear and comprehensive information about expenses as suggested by the Law Explanation. The original text mandates following company guidelines while the modified text suggests they 'should be considered,' reducing the binding nature of those guidelines. Furthermore, the original text requires providing evidence of expenses 'where reasonably practicable', whereas the changed text makes it optional ('if you find it convenient'). This cumulative weakening of the expense reimbursement terms creates an ambiguity regarding the company's obligation, potentially leading to disputes and non-compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 alludes to the Employment Rights Act 1996, which although is not specifically cited with any content in the excerpt, is the legislation being questioned in the law explanation. The changes collectively undermine the employee's right to clear terms regarding expense reimbursement, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to your compliance with the Company's sickness absence procedures (as amended from time to time), the Company may in its sole and absolute discretion pay full salary and contractual benefits during any period of absence due to sickness or injury for up to an aggregate of 3 months in any fifty-two (52) week period (whether such absence is continuous or intermittent in any calendar year). Such payment shall be inclusive of any statutory sick pay due in accordance with applicable legislation in force at the time of absence. The Company may, in its sole and absolute discretion, extend the period of allowance in an individual case if the circumstances so justify. Thereafter, the Company shall pay statutory sick pay or equivalent benefit to which you may be entitled subject to your compliance with the appropriate rules.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to your general adherence to the Company's suggested sickness absence guidelines, the Company might, at its consideration, provide some salary continuation and contractual benefits during sickness, but the duration and extent of this support are not guaranteed and are subject to ongoing review. The Company might consider extending this support, subject to its evolving policies. Subsequently, the Company will aim to provide legally mandated sick pay, conditional on meeting requirements.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a discretionary sick pay period of up to 3 months. The modified text makes the sick pay even more uncertain by replacing 'sole and absolute discretion' with vague terms like 'might' and 'some salary continuation,' and introducing an 'ongoing review'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) under the Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992
perturbation.law_citation: Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992, Part XI
perturbation.law_explanation: The Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992 mandates that employers pay Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) to eligible employees. The modified text's vague language about salary continuation and its emphasis on 'aiming to provide legally mandated sick pay' could be interpreted as an attempt to avoid or obscure the employer's legal obligation to pay SSP, thus contradicting the law. Furthermore, the official government website explains that SSP is a legal entitlement and defines the eligibility requirements.

Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) Skip contents Overview You can get £118.75 per week Statutory Sick Pay ( SSP ) if you’re too ill to work. It’s paid by your employer for up to 28 weeks. This guide is also available in Welsh (Cymraeg) . You cannot get less than the statutory amount. You can get more if your company has a sick pay scheme (or ‘occupational scheme’) - check your employment contract. There are different sick pay rules for agricultural workers . perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the 'sole and absolute discretion' for sick pay with vague terms such as 'might' and 'some salary continuation,' while also adding that the 'duration and extent of this support are not guaranteed'. This contradicts the employer's obligation to pay statutory sick pay (SSP) under the Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992, as described in the Law Explanation. While the original contract provides a discretionary sick pay for up to 3 months, the modified contract creates ambiguity around legally mandated sick pay. The scraped snippet from GOV.UK regarding Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) states, 'You can get £118.75 per week Statutory Sick Pay ( SSP ) if you’re too ill to work. It’s paid by your employer for up to 28 weeks. You cannot get less than the statutory amount.' The modified text's shift to 'aiming to provide legally mandated sick pay, conditional on meeting requirements' weakens the employee's entitlement and introduces uncertainty, contradicting the law's requirement that employees 'cannot get less than the statutory amount.'
